Knowledge graphs represent information as interconnected nodes and edges, where each node corresponds to a concept or entity and each edge denotes a relationship between them.
This structured representation enables machines to traverse relationships, answer complex queries, and discover implicit connections that would be difficult to identify in unstructured text alone. Modern graph databases such as Neo4j and Amazon Neptune provide efficient storage and traversal engines for these networks.

Retrieval-augmented generation combines the strengths of large language models with external knowledge retrieval to produce more accurate and grounded responses.
	By first fetching relevant passages from a vector store or graph database, the system supplies the language model with factual context, reducing hallucinations and improving citation quality. This two-stage architecture has become a cornerstone of enterprise AI applications.
 RAG pipelines typically include an embedding step, a nearest-neighbor search, and a final synthesis phase that merges retrieved evidence with the user query.

Memory-augmented workflows extend traditional data pipelines by maintaining persistent state across multiple processing stages. Rather than treating each document in isolation, these workflows accumulate episodic and semantic memories that inform future extraction and reasoning steps. M-Flow leverages this approach to build incremental knowledge representations, allowing the system to refine its understanding as new data arrives.
 The episodic memory layer captures temporal sequences of events, while the semantic layer distills durable facts and relationships.

Vector embeddings map high-dimensional data such as text, images, or audio into dense numerical representations that preserve semantic similarity. Cosine similarity and dot-product metrics are commonly used to rank candidates during nearest-neighbor search, enabling sub-linear retrieval times even on corpora containing millions of documents.

Pipeline orchestration frameworks coordinate multi-step data transformations by defining tasks, dependencies, and retry policies in a declarative manner. Whether running on a single machine or across a distributed cluster, the orchestrator schedules work units, monitors progress, and handles failures gracefully. Modern systems support dynamic branching, conditional execution, and real-time progress reporting, making them suitable for both batch ETL workloads and interactive knowledge-graph construction scenarios.
